title: Apply: Birthright Citizenship and Protection of Freedom
description: Apply your knowledge about section one of the Fourteenth Amendment in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Fourteenth Amendment: Citizenship, Freedom, and Equality
Apply: Birthright Citizenship and Protection of Freedom

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1866 letter to the editor of the *New York Times*, “The National Question: The Constitutional Amendments—National Citizenship,” written under the [[☃ definition 1]] Madison.**

What the rights and privileges of a citizen of the United States are, are thus summed up in another case: Protection by the Government; the enjoyment of life and liberty, with the rights to possess and acquire property of every kind, and to pursue and obtain happiness and safety;  to institute and maintain actions of any kind in the Courts of the State. These are the long-defined rights of a citizen of the United States, with which States cannot constitutionally interfere. And it is because they were generally denied to the whole class, on account of color, that further legislation became necessary.

**Which event is responsible for rights being “denied to the whole class, on account of color,” as described in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a fake name used by a writer

choices.content: The ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment
The implementation of Black Codes
The issuing of the Emancipation Proclamation
The Supreme Court’s decision in *United States v. Wong Kim Ark*

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1866 letter to the editor of the *New York Times*, “The National Question: The Constitutional Amendments—National Citizenship,” written under the [[☃ definition 1]] Madison.**

What the rights and privileges of a citizen of the United States are, are thus summed up in another case: Protection by the Government; the enjoyment of life and liberty, with the rights to possess and acquire property of every kind, and to pursue and obtain happiness and safety;  to institute and maintain actions of any kind in the Courts of the State. These are the long-defined rights of a citizen of the United States, with which States cannot constitutionally interfere. And it is because they were generally denied to the whole class, on account of color, that further legislation became necessary.

**According to the excerpt, why was new legislation “needed” to secure the rights of citizens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a fake name used by a writer

choices.content: The Constitution did not guarantee freedom for all people.
The federal government refused to respect the laws of the states.
The Constitution did not list the rights for citizens.
States refused to give people of certain races their rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Field’s dissenting opinion in the *Slaughterhouse Cases* (1873).*

[The Fourteenth Amendment’s Citizenship Clause] recognizes in [[☃ definition 1]] terms, if it does not create, citizens of the United States, and it makes their citizenship dependent upon the place of their birth, or the fact of their adoption, and not upon the constitution or laws of any State or the condition of their ancestry.

**Which statement best summarizes Field’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Citizenship Clause limits citizenship to people whose parents are American citizens or who are adopted by American citizens after their birth.
The Citizenship Clause establishes that citizenship is determined by where a person is born or by naturalization, not by state rules or family background.
The Citizenship Clause implies that people born only in certain states are citizens of the United States.
The Citizenship Clause explicitly states which people can become citizens of the United States, limiting citizenship to people of a shared ancestry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Field’s dissenting opinion in the *Slaughterhouse Cases* (1873).*

[The Fourteenth Amendment’s Citizenship Clause] recognizes in [[☃ definition 1]] terms, if it does not create, citizens of the United States, and it makes their citizenship dependent upon the place of their birth, or the fact of their adoption, and not upon the constitution or laws of any State or the condition of their ancestry.

**The argument in this excerpt most closely reflects the argument in which later Supreme Court case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *United States v. Wong Kim Ark* (1898)
*Lochner v. New York* (1905)
*Griswold v. Connecticut* (1965)
*Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization* (2022)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Griswold v. Connecticut* (1965), written by Justice William O. Douglas.**

Various guarantees create zones of privacy. The right of association contained in the [[☃ definition 1]] of the First Amendment is one . . . . The Third Amendment in its prohibition against the quartering of soldiers ‘in any house’ in time of peace without the consent of the owner is another [[☃ definition 3]] of that privacy. The Fourth Amendment explicitly affirms the ‘right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures.’ The Fifth Amendment in its Self-Incrimination Clause enables the citizen to create a zone of privacy which government may not force him to surrender to his [[☃ definition 2]]. The Ninth Amendment provides: ‘The enumeration in the Constitution, of certain rights, shall not be [[☃ definition 4]] to deny or [[☃ definition 5]] others retained by the people.’ . . .

The present case, then concerns a relationship lying within the zone of privacy created by several fundamental constitutional guarantees. . . . Such a law cannot stand in light of the familiar principle, so often applied by this court . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to privacy is limited to what is explicitly laid out in the Bill of Rights.
The right to privacy does not exist in any of the amendments in the Bill of Rights.
The right to privacy is protected only at the state level, not the federal level.
The right to privacy is connected to several rights listed in the Bill of Rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Griswold v. Connecticut* (1965), written by Justice William O. Douglas.**

Various guarantees create zones of privacy. The right of association contained in the [[☃ definition 1]] of the First Amendment is one . . . . The Third Amendment in its prohibition against the quartering of soldiers ‘in any house’ in time of peace without the consent of the owner is another [[☃ definition 3]] of that privacy. The Fourth Amendment explicitly affirms the ‘right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures.’ The Fifth Amendment in its Self-Incrimination Clause enables the citizen to create a zone of privacy which government may not force him to surrender to his [[☃ definition 2]]. The Ninth Amendment provides: ‘The enumeration in the Constitution, of certain rights, shall not be [[☃ definition 4]] to deny or [[☃ definition 5]] others retained by the people.’ . . .

The present case, then concerns a relationship lying within the zone of privacy created by several fundamental constitutional guarantees. . . . Such a law cannot stand in light of the familiar principle, so often applied by this court . . .

**Which statement best describes how the reasoning in the excerpt reflects our current understanding of rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Only rights listed explicitly in the Constitution are protected, and any new rights must be added through an amendment.
Rights that are listed in the Constitution must be interpreted with a modern lens, as life has changed since the Constitution was written.
There are rights not specifically listed in the Constitution that are still protected from government interference.
All rights listed in the Bill of Rights are protected from interference by state governments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Civil Rights Bill” an article written by S.S. Nichols and printed in the *Daily National Intelligencer* on May 8, 1866.**

The bill of rights, or what are termed the guarantees of liberty, contained in the Federal Constitution, have none of them any sort of application or bearing upon the State governments, but are solely [[☃ definition 1]] or restrictions upon the Federal Government. The recent attempt in Congress to treat them as [[☃ definition 2]] against the State governments, with an accompanying [[☃ definition 3]] power to enforce the guaranties, is a surprising evidence of stolid ignorance of Constitutional law, or of a shameless effort to impose upon the ignorant.
[[☃ definition 4]]

**The argument in the excerpt most directly relates to which constitutional principle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: formal promises that certain conditions will be met
likely to happen as a consequence of something

choices.content: Incorporation
Stare decisis
Judicial review
Separation of powers

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Civil Rights Bill” an article written by S.S. Nichols and printed in the *Daily National Intelligencer* on May 8, 1866.**

The bill of rights, or what are termed the guarantees of liberty, contained in the Federal Constitution, have none of them any sort of application or bearing upon the State governments, but are solely [[☃ definition 1]] or restrictions upon the Federal Government. The recent attempt in Congress to treat them as [[☃ definition 2]] against the State governments, with an accompanying [[☃ definition 3]] power to enforce the guaranties, is a surprising evidence of stolid ignorance of Constitutional law, or of a shameless effort to impose upon the ignorant.
[[☃ definition 4]]

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: formal promises that certain conditions will be met
likely to happen as a consequence of something

choices.content: The Bill of Rights only protects the unalienable rights of life, liberty, and property.
The Bill of Rights has always applied to state governments, not just the federal government.
The Bill of Rights applies only to the federal government, not to state governments.
The protections listed in the Bill of Rights are merely suggestions about the limitations of the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania Senator Edgar Cowan’s comments in a debate about the proposed Fourteenth Amendment in May of 1866.**

I am really desirous to have a legal definition of “citizenship of the United States.” What does it mean? What is its length and breadth? I would be glad if the honorable Senator[[☃ definition 1]] would favor us with some such definition. Is the child of the Chinese immigrant in California a citizen? Is the child of a [[☃ definition 2]] born in Pennsylvania a citizen? If so, what rights have they? Have they any more rights than a [[☃ definition 3]] in the United States? If a traveler comes here from Ethiopia, from Australia, or Great Britain, he is entitled, to a certain extent, to the protection of the laws. . . . He has a right to the protection of the laws; but he is not a citizen in the ordinary acceptation of the word. . . 

**Which statement best summarizes the objection Senator Cowan makes in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: in a sincere and serious way
a now-offensive term for someone of Romani descent

choices.content: The Fourteenth Amendment does not adequately describe the protections tourists have when they visit the country.
The Fourteenth Amendment has not developed a naturalization process or a clear way for immigrants to become citizens.
The Fourteenth Amendment does not define who can become citizens of the United States, potentially including the children of immigrants.
The Fourteenth Amendment would interfere with the states’ right to determine who becomes a citizen of their state.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania Senator Edgar Cowan’s comments in a debate about the proposed Fourteenth Amendment in May of 1866.**

I am really desirous to have a legal definition of “citizenship of the United States.” What does it mean? What is its length and breadth? I would be glad if the honorable Senator [[☃ definition 1]] would favor us with some such definition. Is the child of the Chinese immigrant in California a citizen? Is the child of a [[☃ definition 2]] born in Pennsylvania a citizen? If so, what rights have they? Have they any more rights than a [[☃ definition 3]] in the United States? If a traveler comes here from Ethiopia, from Australia, or Great Britain, he is entitled, to a certain extent, to the protection of the laws. . . . He has a right to the protection of the laws; but he is not a citizen in the ordinary acceptation of the word. . . 

**Which Supreme Court case dealt with the issue Senator Cowan raises in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: in a sincere and serious way
a now-offensive term for someone of Romani descent

choices.content: *Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896)
*United States v. Wong Kim Ark* (1898)
*Lochner v. New York* (1905)
*Griswold v. Connecticut* (1965)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania Senator Edgar Cowan’s comments in a debate about the proposed Fourteenth Amendment in May of 1866.**

I am really desirous to have a legal definition of “citizenship of the United States.” What does it mean? What is its length and breadth? I would be glad if the honorable Senator[[☃ definition 1]] would favor us with some such definition. Is the child of the Chinese immigrant in California a citizen? Is the child of a [[☃ definition 2]] born in Pennsylvania a citizen? If so, what rights have they? Have they any more rights than a [[☃ definition 3]] in the United States? If a traveler comes here from Ethiopia, from Australia, or Great Britain, he is entitled, to a certain extent, to the protection of the laws. . . . He has a right to the protection of the laws; but he is not a citizen in the ordinary acceptation of the word. . . 

**Which section of the Fourteenth Amendment does Senator Cowan object to?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: in a sincere and serious way
a now-offensive term for someone of Romani descent

choices.content: Section 1
Section 2
Section 3
Section 4

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania Representative Thayer comments on the Civil Rights Act of 1866.**

If, then, the freedmen are now citizens, or if we have the constitutional right to make them such, they are clearly entitled to those guarantees of the Constitution of the United States which are intended for the protection of all citizens.

They are entitled to the benefit of that guarantee of the Constitution which secures to every citizen the enjoyment of life, liberty, and property, and no just reason exists why they should not enjoy the protection of that guarantee of the Constitution.

**Which part of the Fourteenth Amendment would best address Thayer’s comments in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Citizenship Clause
The Enforcement Clause
The Full Faith and Credit Clause
The Privileges or Immunities Clause
